items.q: What is the difference between CAD and CAM?
items.correct: CAD creates digital designs; CAM uses those designs to control manufacturing machines.
items.misconception: They are the same thing with different names.
items.random_wrong: CAD is for cars, CAM is for cameras.
items.why: Students treat the terms as synonymous because they often appear together. CAD = drawing/modelling software; CAM = machine control (CNC routers, 3D printers) - typically the second stage.

items.correct: A small integrated chip containing CPU, memory, and I/O for controlling a device.
items.misconception: A very small computer like a laptop.
items.random_wrong: A remote control for a TV.
items.why: Students think microcontrollers are mini-PCs. They are specialised: limited resources, designed for a single embedded task. No operating system needed - just dedicated code running directly.

items.q: What is FEA used for in product design?
items.correct: Simulating how a product will respond to forces, heat, or vibration before manufacture.
items.misconception: Final exam analysis of student designs.
items.random_wrong: Counting the elements in a chemical compound.
items.why: Students unfamiliar with the acronym guess at it. FEA = Finite Element Analysis: dividing a part into many small elements and simulating physical behaviour computationally - saves prototyping costs.

items.q: Which prototype tests how a product functions rather than how it looks?
items.why: Correct answer: Working prototype. “Appearance prototype” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which digital process is most commonly used for rapid prototyping at A-Level?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 3D printing. “Sand casting” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Approximately how many iterative cycles might a school design project undergo at A-Level?
items.why: The real figure is 3 cycles. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
